response:
THE LAW OF MOORE, FORMULATED BY GORDON MOORE IN 1965, PREDICTS THAT THE NUMBER OF TRANSISTORS ON A MICROCHIP DOUBLES EVERY TWO YEARS. THIS TRENDS HAS BEEN A DRIVING FORCE BEHIND THE RAPID ADVANCEMENT OF COMPUTING TECHNOLOGY. HOWEVER, AS WE APPROACH THE PHYSICAL LIMITS OF SILICON-BASED TECHNOLOGY, IT BECOMES INCREASINGLY DIFFICULT TO CONTINUE THIS RATE OF GROWTH. SCIENTISTS AND ENGINEERS ARE NOW EXPLORING ALTERNATIVE MATERIALS AND NEW ARCHITECTURES TO SUSTAIN THE GROWTH OF COMPUTING POWER.

***
WHILE THE LAW OF MOORE MAY NOT CEASE TO HOLD IN ITS ORIGINAL FORM, IT IS LIKELY TO EVOLVE INTO A DIFFERENT PATTERN. INSTEAD OF A STRICT DOUBLING EVERY TWO YEARS, THE RATE OF IMPROVEMENT MAY SLOW DOWN. THIS DOES NOT MEAN THAT TECHNOLOGICAL PROGRESS WILL STOP, BUT RATHER THAT IT WILL NEED TO BE DRIVEN BY INNOVATION IN OTHER AREAS SUCH AS QUANTUM COMPUTING, NEURAL NETWORKS, AND ENERGY EFFICIENCY. THE FUTURE OF COMPUTING WILL DEPEND ON A COMBINATION OF NEW TECHNOLOGIES AND MORE EFFICIENT USE OF EXISTING ONES. COMPUTING DO NOT STOP COMPUTING.
